name: Milk-brotherhood. Friends bound in brotherhood through partaking of milk from the same woman.
additional_description: Friends bound in brotherhood through partaking of milk from the same woman.

name: Friendship starts at babyhood: two babies exchanged.
Combat of disguised friends.
Friends offer to die for each other. (Bürgschaft.) Each falsely confesses crime so as to save the other. Neither guilty. Often combined with P325.
bibliographies: **Abeles "Die Bürgschaft als Motif in der jüdischen Literatur" Monatschr. f. Geschichte u. Wissenschaft der Juden LX 213ff., 263ff.; **K. Kelling Das Bürgschaftsmotiv in der französischen Literatur (Leipzig diss., 1915); *Chauvin III 124 No. 113, V 215f., VIII 194ff. IX 16f.; *Hdwb. d. Märchens I 350a s. v. additional_description: (B'rgschaft.) Each falsely confesses crime so as to save the other. Neither guilty.

name: Competition in friendship: prisoner and jailor. Officer in charge of prison offers to let his friend escape, though his own life will be forfeited. The friend refuses; tells officer to let king think he has escaped and if the king demands his life the officer can produce the prisoner. King hears of the generosity and forgives the prisoner.
additional_description: Officer in charge of prison offers to let his friend escape, though his own life will be forfeited. The friend refuses; tells officer to let king think he has escaped and if the king demands his life the officer can produce the prisoner. King hears of the generosity and forgives the prisoner.

name: Friend gives false witness to set free his accused friend.
Friend sacrifices his life for the other.
Man knowing of murder plot against his friend disguises and is killed in his place. (Cf. P361.1.)
bibliographies: Scala Celi 9b No. 61; Spanish Exempla: Keller.

name: Refusal to believe that a friend has spoken ill of one.
Refusal to believe that a friend will harm one. Alexander drinks cup said to have been poisoned by his friend.
additional_description: Alexander drinks cup said to have been poisoned by his friend.

name: Man refuses to follow friend in wicked conduct.
bibliographies: Scala Celi 11a No. 66; Alphabet No. 56; Italian Novella: Rotunda.

name: Deeds of friendship – miscellaneous.
Two friends captured by Moors have money to ransom only one. The ransomed one returns home, gets money and buys the other's freedom.
additional_description: The ransomed one returns home, gets money and buys the other's freedom.

name: Man who has counseled friend in assassination asks to be killed on the other's body.
Friend's intercession saves man from execution.
The sacred partnership. Man is abducted by pirates and kept in slavery forty years. Upon his return his friend divides his earnings with him.
additional_description: Man is abducted by pirates and kept in slavery forty years. Upon his return his friend divides his earnings with him.

name: Hands of friends extend through sides of tombs and clasp in death.
Successful rival gives his lady to unsuccessful friend.
"Friendship without refusal." Friends bind themselves each to grant every desire of the other.
additional_description: Friends bind themselves each to grant every desire of the other.

name: Danger of one saint voluntarily incurred by another.
Hospitality. Relation of host and guest.
additional_description: Relation of host and guest.

name: Hospitality for a whole winter.
Hospitality for (three) years.
Salt of hospitality. Eating a man's salt creates mutual obligation.
additional_description: Eating a man's salt creates mutual obligation.

name: Guest given refuge. Murderer of a man's father takes refuge in his house and is saved by him.
additional_description: Murderer of a man's father takes refuge in his house and is saved by him.

name: Nobleman forces escaping prisoner to accept his hospitality. Intercedes for his pardon.
additional_description: Intercedes for his pardon.

name: Guest in disguise or under false name. (Cf. K1831.)
Refugee entertained in holy place (church, monastery, etc.).
Hosts refrain from telling guest of death in household.
bibliographies: Wesselski Archiv Orientální II 431; Greek: Euripides Alcestis.

name: Host greets guest with gifts.
bibliographies: English romance: Malory passim; Icelandic: Boberg.

name: Host treats guest with food and everything possible.
Guests fed before being questioned.
Guests' life inviolable.

Host surrenders his wife to his guest. The guest unwittingly falls in love with the wife. The host, on being informed, out of pure generosity repudiates the wife and has her marry the guest. (Often joined with P315.)
bibliographies: Chauvin V 136 No. 64; also references to P315; Spanish Exempla: Keller.
additional_description: The guest unwittingly falls in love with the wife. The host, on being informed, out of pure generosity repudiates the wife and has her marry the guest.

name: Guest begets son with his host's daughter.
If host does not return, the house shall belong to the guest. So declares the host as he departs on a mission for the guest.
additional_description: So declares the host as he departs on a mission for the guest.

name: Barmecide feast. Host places imaginary feast before guest, who accepts it in the same spirit. Guest's courtesy is rewarded by real feast.
bibliographies: *Chauvin V 163 No. 86; Arabian: Burton Nights I 317.
additional_description: Host places imaginary feast before guest, who accepts it in the same spirit. Guest's courtesy is rewarded by real feast.

name: Strangers entertained by family to whose hitching-ring they happen to tie their horses. Thus confusion avoided as to where strangers are to be entertained.
bibliographies: Italian: L. de Francia Novellino (Torino, 1930), Rotunda.
additional_description: Thus confusion avoided as to where strangers are to be entertained.

name: Refusal to receive preferred help until series of stories has been told.
Selfish guest expels host. Porcupine asks rabbit for hospitality. When rabbit complains of being pricked, porcupine tells him to leave if he does not like it.
additional_description: Porcupine asks rabbit for hospitality. When rabbit complains of being pricked, porcupine tells him to leave if he does not like it.

name: Shabby hospitality forces guests to leave.

bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: MADRAS . Natesa Sastri Folklore 128 .

Poor host and his wife kill themselves because they are unable to entertain expected guests.

name: Wife scolds husband's hospitality, as he really has nothing to give.
Poor peasant closes the eyes in order not to see guest eat: later suicide.

Christian king makes baptism a condition for hospitality during the winter.
Sitting in a circle of feasts.
Teacher and pupil.
Druids as teachers.
Teacher dies of pride over success of pupil.
Student enters competition with his master.
Student challenges his fencing master. Is overcome by the latter's tricks.
additional_description: Is overcome by the latter's tricks.

name: Faithful servant kills his master's murderer and is killed in turn.

Brave soldier and timid cabinet-maker as companions.

name: Why shoemakers are indolent. A shoemaker spits at Christ on way to be crucified. Christ tells him, "A poor slobbering fellow thou shalt be, and all shoemakers after thee, for what thou has done to me." (Cf. A2231.2, P445.1.)
additional_description: A shoemaker spits at Christ on way to be crucified. Christ tells him, "A poor slobbering fellow thou shalt be, and all shoemakers after thee, for what thou has done to me."

Treacherous philosophers.

Pardoning of criminal comes too late.
Youngest of judges first to give decision.
Crime less serious if committed at request of a lady.
Cities of refuge.

Complacent judge disregards the confession. He has put the criminal to torture without success. When he releases him, the criminal says, "In a moment I should have confessed all." The judge lets him go nevertheless.
additional_description: He has put the criminal to torture without success. When he releases him, the criminal says, "In a moment I should have confessed all." The judge lets him go nevertheless.

name: Laws.
An eye for an eye , a tooth for a tooth NILGIRI . Kota : Emeneau.
Lex talionis. One life for one life. Equal number must be given up by each feuding side. (Cf. P535.)
bibliographies: Irish myth: Cross (P548.2); India: Thompson-Balys: NILGIRI . Kota : Emeneau .; New Guinea: Ker 61.
additional_description: One life for one life. Equal number must be given up by each feuding side.

"It is a debt if it is promised."
Contract made by madman void. (Cf. P192.)
Contract made by woman without her husband void.
He nearest to blood of slain man must avenge his death.
Legal principles.
"To every cow belongs its calf," a legal principle applied to question of ownership of copy of manuscript.
"To every son belongs his mother": in case of suspected illegitimacy, child is not guilty.
Taxation and payment of fines or tribute.
Tribe failing to attend yearly feast to send gift as sign of submission.
